---
title: "Methods in R"
subtitle: "A topic reference for biomedical research and peer review"
author: "R. Heller"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
documentclass: book
bibliography: [references.bib, packages.bib]
biblio-style: apalike
csl: style/vancouver.csl
link-citations: yes
github-repo: r-heller/methods-in-r
url: https://r-heller.github.io/methods-in-r/
description: "A bench reference for biomedical authors and peer reviewers — every common method covered with R code, diagnostics, and reviewer-facing red flags."
cover-image: "images/cover.png"
---

# Welcome! {-}

```{r cover, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, out.width = "280px", fig.align = "left", fig.alt = "Methods in R book cover"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/cover.png")
```

Welcome to *Methods in R*, a topic reference for biomedical research and peer review.

## About this book {-}

The R / biostatistics shelf is crowded with linear textbooks. *Methods in R* is shaped differently: it expects to serve two readers in the same week — the **author** who has data in front of them and needs to pick a method, run it correctly in R, and write the Methods paragraph; and the **peer reviewer** who has a manuscript on their desk and needs to know what a competent application of the method looks like, which diagnostics should have been reported, and what red flags to flag.

Every method page therefore ends with a *For Reviewers* section: the misapplications, the missing diagnostics, the red flags in tables and figures, and the things to verify against the authors' references and arithmetic. The book opens at Chapter \@ref(sec-find-your-method), a decision tree from research question to method.

The body covers sixteen topic areas — from foundations and inference through regression, survival, Bayesian methods, machine learning, bioinformatics, and study design. Hands-on labs consolidate the methods of each area into a single runnable file. A final part, *Reporting Templates*, gives copy-paste Methods and Results paragraphs tagged by **STROBE / CONSORT / TRIPOD-AI / PRISMA / ARRIVE**.

After reading, you should be able to (a) pick a defensible method for a given research question, (b) run it in R with diagnostics you can defend at peer review, and (c) write up a Methods paragraph that meets the relevant reporting standard.

## How to use this book {-}

- **Linear vs. lookup.** If you already know the method, jump to its chapter. If not, start at Chapter \@ref(sec-find-your-method) and follow the decision tree.
- **Where the code lives.** Code in each method page and lab is display-only in the rendered book; copy it into an R session with the packages cited in that section attached. Reusable helpers live in `R/helpers.R` in the source repository.
- **Corrections and suggestions.** Open an issue at <https://github.com/r-heller/methods-in-r/issues>.
- **Per-chapter PDFs.** Every chapter has a *Download this chapter (PDF)* button at the top of its page (rendered alongside the HTML on each push to `main`).
- **Whole-book PDF and EPUB.** Use the *Download* menu in the navbar (top of every page) for a single-file PDF or EPUB of the whole book.

## License {-}

The book content is released under [CC BY-SA 4.0](LICENSE-CONTENT); source code under the [MIT License](LICENSE). Suggested attribution: *R. Heller, Methods in R*.

## Citation {-}

See the dedicated [Citing this Guide](citing-this-guide.html) chapter for the prose citation, BibTeX, and RIS download.

## Acknowledgments {-}

Tooling: [bookdown](https://bookdown.org/), R Core, and the wider R ecosystem. Source material consolidated from the MIT-licensed `CTTIR/courses` and `CTTIR/tutorials` repositories (see the dedicated [Acknowledgments](acknowledgments.html) page for the full statement, including LLM-tool use).

```{r packages-bib, include = FALSE, eval = TRUE}
knitr::write_bib(c(.packages(), "bookdown", "knitr", "rmarkdown"),
                 "packages.bib")
```
